gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Directed evolution (DE) is inefficient when mutations exhibit epistasis. The paper introduces Active Learning-assisted Directed Evolution (ALDE), an iterative ML-assisted DE workflow that uses uncertainty quantification to explore protein sequence space more efficiently than standard DE. The goal is to optimize protein fitness, particularly for challenging landscapes with multiple epistatic residues.

section_gists.heading: Active learning-assisted directed evolution
section_gists.gist: Introduces ALDE as a solution to DE's inefficiency with epistatic mutations. DE is greedy hill climbing, limited to local regions and prone to local optima. ML, especially active learning with uncertainty quantification, can navigate complex landscapes better. ALDE alternates between wet-lab screening and ML model training to propose new sequences. The paper demonstrates ALDE on a 5-residue enzyme optimization problem, improving yield from 12% to 93% in three rounds, and validates with simulations on GB1 and TrpB datasets.
process_signals: DE is limited to local search and gets stuck at local optima on rugged landscapes.
Active learning leverages uncertainty to balance exploration and exploitation.
ALDE is designed for batch protein engineering with tens to hundreds of sequences per round.
methods: Iterative batch Bayesian optimization (BO) with surrogate models and acquisition functions.
Combinatorial design space on k residues (here k=5).
Initial random library, then model training and acquisition for subsequent rounds.
metrics: Fitness: quantitative measurement of efficacy (e.g., yield, selectivity).
Objective: cis – trans Yield for cyclopropanation.
failures_or_limits: DE fails when mutations are non-additive.
MLDE (ML-assisted DE) is limited to small design spaces and does not use iterative uncertainty.

section_gists.heading: Practical implementation of ALDE
section_gists.gist: ALDE workflow: define combinatorial design space on k residues (20^k variants). Start with simultaneous mutagenesis and initial screening. Train ML model on sequence-fitness data, apply acquisition function to rank all variants, select top N for next round. Repeat until fitness optimized. Compatible with low-N batch settings. Code available.
process_signals: Choice of k balances epistasis capture vs. data requirements.
Initial round uses random variants; subsequent rounds use model predictions.
Acquisition function balances exploration and exploitation.
methods: Combinatorial library synthesis via NNK degenerate codons.
Exact gene synthesis for subsequent rounds.
ML model training with uncertainty quantification.
Acquisition functions: Greedy, UCB, Thompson Sampling.
failures_or_limits: Larger k may require more data.

section_gists.heading: The active site of ParPgb is a challenging design space for standard DE
section_gists.gist: Target: ParPgb enzyme for cyclopropanation of 4-vinylanisole with EDA, aiming for high cis yield and selectivity. Parent ParLQ (W59L V60Q) has moderate yield (~40%) and trans preference (3:1). Five active-site residues (W56, Y57, L59, Q60, F89) chosen based on known epistasis. Single-site saturation mutagenesis (SSM) showed no significant improvement in objective (cis-trans yield). Recombination of best single mutants (DAYFW, DGMDW, DGMVW) also failed to improve objective, confirming epistasis and challenging DE.
process_signals: SSM revealed no single mutation significantly improves objective.
Recombination of best single mutants did not yield effective proteins, indicating strong epistasis.
F89Y mutation important for cis selectivity but only in specific contexts.
metrics: Related metrics: cis Yield, cis/trans Selectivity.
failures_or_limits: SSM failed to identify beneficial single mutations.
Naive recombination of best single mutants failed.
Zero-shot predictors not correlated with objective (non-native chemistry).

section_gists.heading: Using ALDE to efficiently optimize ParPgb for a non-native carbene transfer reaction
section_gists.gist: ALDE campaign: Initial random library of 216 unique variants (NNK mutagenesis, LevSeq sequencing). Most variants active but trans-selective; some with high cis yield. F89Y key for cis selectivity. Round 1: DNN ensemble with one-hot encoding and Thompson sampling selected top 90 sequences for synthesis and screening. Best variant MKFNY: 93% total yield, 12:1 cis:trans. Round 2: model retrained, top 90 sequences again; best MPFDY: 99% yield, 14:1 cis:trans. Model explored more in Round 2. Mutations in MPFDY not obvious from SSM; they work together. Chiral GC showed high enantioselectivity. ALDE also produced variants with broader substrate scope.
process_signals: Initial random library showed most variants active but trans-selective; some cis-selective variants emerged.
F89Y mutation critical for cis selectivity but context-dependent.
Round 1: nearly 1/3 of sequences better than best initial variant.
Round 2: model explored more, increased variance in activities.
Best variant MPFDY had mutations not individually beneficial.
ALDE yielded variants with broader substrate scope than single optimized enzyme.
methods: Exact DNA synthesis for Rounds 1 and 2.
DNN ensemble (5 models, bootstrapping) with one-hot encoding.
failures_or_limits: Initial random library had no strong correlation with zero-shot predictors.
Some sequences in Round 2 had lower performance due to exploration.

section_gists.heading: Computational simulations on combinatorial protein datasets support the utility of ALDE
section_gists.gist: Simulations on GB1 and TrpB (4-residue combinatorial landscapes, ~160k variants each). Baseline: DE greedy walk (mutate one residue at a time, fix best, repeat). ALDE simulation: batch BO with 96 initial random samples, 4 rounds of 96 samples each. Tested encodings (AAIndex, Georgiev, Onehot, ESM2), models (GP, DKL, DNN ensemble, Boosting ensemble), acquisition functions (Greedy, UCB, TS). ALDE significantly outperforms average DE and random sampling. DNN ensemble with one-hot and Thompson sampling most consistent. Uncertainty quantification analysis: frequentist ensembles (DNN, boosting) better calibrated than GP/DKL. Calibration metrics: MAE, miscalibration area, Spearman correlation between uncertainty and error. Underconfident/overconfident models identified.
process_signals: ALDE outperforms DE and random sampling across datasets.
DNN ensemble with one-hot and TS most consistent.
Frequentist ensembles better calibrated than Bayesian GP/DKL.
Uncertainty quantification plays a more limited role than expected.
methods: DE greedy walk simulation (all starting points, all residue orders).
Batch BO simulation mimicking wet-lab campaign.
Models: GP, DKL, DNN ensemble, Boosting ensemble.
metrics: Max fitness achieved (normalized to global max).
Calibration: MAE, miscalibration area, Spearman correlation.
failures_or_limits: GP and DKL models showed poorer calibration.
ESM2 encoding did not consistently outperform simpler encodings.
qEI acquisition function ran slowly without improvement.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: ALDE advantages: handles epistasis, avoids local optima, broader substrate scope. Simulations show ALDE outperforms MLDE and DE. Frequentist ensembles work best; classical uncertainty quantification less critical than expected. Wet-lab campaign: 6 plates total (4 random, 2 predicted) vs. ~5 rounds for DE greedy walk. Enabled by LevSeq and rapid gene synthesis. ALDE generalizable to other proteins and larger design spaces, but residue selection remains a challenge. Open questions: scaling with number of residues, effect of epistasis, integration with generative models for large spaces.
process_signals: ALDE can find combinations with desirable epistatic effects.
Frequentist ensembles outperformed Bayesian models in practice.
Uncertainty quantification played a more limited role than expected.
ALDE serendipitously produced variants with broader substrate scope.
Wet-lab campaign required less screening than estimated DE greedy walk.
failures_or_limits: Proof of advantage over DE not possible without exhaustive wet-lab testing.
Residue selection for design space is an open challenge.
Scaling to more residues may require more data; relationship unknown.
Generative modeling may be needed if design space cannot be enumerated.

section_gists.heading: Machine learning details
section_gists.gist: Wet-lab campaign: training data from merged sequencing and screening yields, normalized to parent cis yield. Model: one-hot encoding, DNN ensemble (5 models, bootstrapping with 90% data), Thompson sampling. Same protocol as simulations. Code available. BO components: probabilistic surrogate model and acquisition function.
process_signals: Design choices based on most consistent strategy from simulations.
Normalization to parent cis yield on each plate.
methods: BO loop: surrogate model updated each iteration.

section_gists.heading: Probabilistic models for bayesian optimization
section_gists.gist: Four surrogate models: GP (with Matern or SE kernel, hyperparameters via marginal likelihood), DKL (GP on DNN features), Boosting ensemble (5 models, sequential error correction, bootstrapping), DNN ensemble (5 identical architectures, different initializations, bootstrapping). DNN ensembles lack proper Bayesian interpretation but can be seen as approximate Bayesian inference.
process_signals: GP performs poorly in high-dimensional input spaces.
DKL addresses GP limitations by learning features.
Boosting and DNN ensembles are frequentist but used for uncertainty via bootstrapping.
methods: DNN ensemble: 5 MLPs, bootstrapping with 90% data.
failures_or_limits: GP struggles with high-dimensional encodings like ESM2.
DNN ensembles lack proper Bayesian interpretation.

section_gists.heading: Acquisition functions for bayesian optimization
section_gists.gist: Three acquisition functions: Expected Improvement (EI) (batch qEI, but slow, not used), Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) (μ + β^(1/2)σ, β=4, batch by top q values), Thompson Sampling (TS) (sample from posterior, maximize; for GP/DKL use 1000 random Fourier features; for ensembles, pick one model). Greedy is UCB with β=0. TS is stochastic but less so with small ensembles.
process_signals: qEI was tested but ran slowly without improvement, so excluded.
UCB uses heuristic batch selection (top q).
TS uses independent samples for batch; less stochastic with 5-model ensemble.
methods: EI: E[{f(x)-f*}^+], batch via greedy sequential.
TS: sample function from posterior, maximize.
For ensembles, μ and σ from ensemble mean/std.
failures_or_limits: qEI computationally expensive and not beneficial.
TS less stochastic with small ensembles.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: ALDE was developed to overcome DE's limitations with epistatic mutations by using active learning with uncertainty quantification.
The ParPgb active site was chosen as a challenging test case because SSM and recombination failed, indicating strong epistasis.
Computational simulations on GB1 and TrpB were used to select the best model/encoding/acquisition combination before wet-lab experiments.
The wet-lab campaign validated that ALDE can efficiently find optimal combinations of epistatic mutations that DE would miss.
Frequentist ensembles (DNN) with simple one-hot encoding and Thompson sampling were found to be most effective, contrary to expectations that Bayesian models or complex encodings would be superior.
ALDE not only optimized the primary objective but also serendipitously produced variants with broader substrate scope, an emergent advantage.
uncertainties_to_resolve: How does ALDE scale with more residues? Is the relationship linear or exponential?
How generally applicable are the findings to other enzymatic systems and fitness landscapes?
What is the optimal strategy for selecting residues to target in the design space?
Can generative models extend ALDE to design spaces too large to enumerate?
Why did frequentist ensembles outperform Bayesian models in uncertainty quantification and performance?
How critical is calibrated uncertainty quantification for ALDE's success in practice?

Round 2: model explored more sequence space; best variant MPFDY achieved 99% total yield and 14:1 cis:trans selectivity, with mutations not individually beneficial.
Substrate scope screening showed Round 2 variants generally outperformed parent ParLQ across eight styrene derivatives, with different top variants per substrate.

We conclude that active learning can significantly outperform the average performance of DE and random sampling, and results are consistent across the two different protein datasets.

Higher dimensional encodings (Onehot and ESM2) generally work better with deep learning-based models (DNN Ensemble and DKL), while non-deep learning models might learn better from low dimensional AAI index and Georgiev parameters.
We find that ESM2 encodings cannot be used by GPs, likely because they are too high dimensional.
Overall, the frequentist ensemble models perform the most consistently across different encodings.
DNN ensembles have the lowest miscalibration areas, suggesting that they are the most calibrated and best models overall.
However, in this study, we find that performance in ALDE simulations (by max fitness achieved) is not necessarily correlated to how calibrated the uncertainties are for each model.

Most Bayesian optimization algorithms consist of two main components: (1) a probabilistic surrogate model of the objective function and (2) an acquisition function.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Directed evolution (DE) is inefficient when mutations exhibit epistasis, limiting exploration to local regions and causing local optima. The paper introduces Active Learning-assisted Directed Evolution (ALDE), an iterative ML-assisted DE workflow that uses uncertainty quantification to explore protein sequence space more efficiently than standard DE.

canonical_events.summary: DE is inefficient when mutations exhibit epistasis, limiting exploration to local regions and causing local optima.
Active learning with uncertainty quantification can explore protein sequence space more efficiently than DE.
ALDE workflow: define combinatorial design space on k residues, start with simultaneous mutagenesis and initial screening, train ML model, use acquisition function to propose new variants, repeat.
Single-site saturation mutagenesis (SSM) at five active-site residues showed no significant improvement in objective.
Recombination of best single mutants (DAYFW, DGMDW, DGMVW) did not yield effective proteins, confirming epistasis.
The active site of ParPgb is a challenging design space for standard DE due to epistasis.
Initial ALDE library constructed via NNK mutagenesis at all five positions, with random selection for screening because zero-shot predictors were not correlated with the objective.
Screening of initial 216-member library revealed most variants active but trans-selective; F89Y mutation critical for cis selectivity in specific contexts.
ALDE computational package used DNN ensemble with one-hot encoding and Thompson sampling, chosen based on extensive computational simulations.
Round 1: top 90 sequences synthesized and screened; best variant MKFNY achieved 93% total yield and 12:1 cis:trans selectivity.
Round 2: model explored more sequence space; best variant MPFDY achieved 99% total yield and 14:1 cis:trans selectivity, with mutations not individually beneficial.
Substrate scope screening showed Round 2 variants generally outperformed parent ParLQ across eight styrene derivatives, with different top variants per substrate.
Simulations on GB1 and TrpB show ALDE significantly outperforms average DE and random sampling; DNN ensemble with one-hot and Thompson sampling most consistent.
Classical uncertainty quantification plays a more limited role than expected; calibration does not necessarily correlate with ALDE performance.

The computational simulations validate the choice of DNN ensemble with one-hot and Thompson sampling.
The simulations reveal that uncertainty calibration is less critical than expected, refining the understanding of ALDE's mechanisms.

initial_request.research_problem: Directed evolution (DE) is inefficient when mutations exhibit epistasis, limiting exploration to local regions and causing local optima.
initial_request.target_object: ParPgb protoglobin for non-native cyclopropanation reaction
initial_request.known_context: DE can be inefficient when mutations exhibit non-additive, or epistatic, behavior. DE is limited because screening for performance can only explore a small, local region of sequence space. Taking one mutational step at a time can cause the experiment to become stuck at a local optimum, especially on rugged protein fitness landscapes where mutation effects exhibit epistasis.
constraints: Combinatorial design space on five active-site residues (W56, Y57, L59, Q60, F89)

Achieve high cis:trans selectivity for cis-2a

Overall, the frequentist ensemble models perform the most consistently across different encodings.

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Classical uncertainty quantification plays a more limited role than expected; calibration does not necessarily correlate with ALDE performance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze miscalibration areas and correlation with max fitness achieved.
evolution_trajectory.observation: DNN ensembles have lowest miscalibration areas, but performance in ALDE simulations is not necessarily correlated to calibration; classical uncertainty quantification plays limited role.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude ALDE effectiveness and limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: DNN ensembles have the lowest miscalibration areas, suggesting that they are the most calibrated and best models overall.
However, in this study, we find that performance in ALDE simulations (by max fitness achieved) is not necessarily correlated to how calibrated the uncertainties are for each model.
Overall, classical notions of uncertainty quantification seem to play a more limited role than expected in these real-world applications.

validation_methods: Wet-lab screening of Round 1 and Round 2 variants
Substrate scope screening on eight styrene derivatives
Computational simulations on GB1 and TrpB fitness landscapes
success_verification.final_conclusion: ALDE successfully improved total yield from 12% to 99% and cis:trans selectivity to 14:1 after exploring only ~0.01% of the design space. Simulations confirm ALDE outperforms average DE and random sampling.
limitations: Classical uncertainty quantification plays a more limited role than expected; calibration does not necessarily correlate with ALDE performance.
